data.title: The sneaker reselling math nobody runs.
data.subtitle: What it actually costs to flip a Jordan drop — and the margin left after every line item.
data.author: StellarAIO

data.explainer: Of a typical $400 sneaker resale, gross profit is ~44%. Then platform fees, shipping, proxy and bot subscriptions, taxes, and the inevitable failed cooks eat another 18–25%. The number that actually lands in your bank account is closer to 20% — and only on the pairs that didn't sit dead-stock.

items.title: Failed cook tax
items.body: Bot fires, card declines, account flags — every miss costs proxy time.

items.title: Dead-stock holding
items.body: Pairs that don't sell in 30 days tie up cash and shelf space.

items.title: Sales tax
items.body: Reseller marketplaces report 1099-Ks at $600+. Plan for it.

items.title: Restock fees
items.body: Returns cost 15–20% in some categories — read the policy.

items.title: Authentication delays
items.body: Slow payouts compound when you cop in volume.

data.title: Four checks before you buy the drop.

steps.title: Pull the resale comp
steps.body: Last 30-day average on StockX or GOAT, not the all-time high.

steps.title: Subtract platform fees
steps.body: 9% sale + $5 payout on StockX. Closer to 13% net.

steps.title: Deduct bot share
steps.body: Subscription + proxies ÷ expected cops this cycle.

steps.title: Floor your margin
steps.body: If after all that the margin is under 25%, skip the drop.

data.text: The bot is the cheapest part of the stack. What kills new resellers is paying for proxies they don't use and hyping pairs that sit dead-stock for 90 days.

data.eyebrow: Run the math, then run the bot
data.title: Stop guessing margin. Start tracking it.
data.subtitle: StellarAIO ships with a backtesting window so you can replay every drop you missed.
